metadata.title: Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial: A Multi-Methodological Analysis of the Discourse of Watts up with That

abstract: Using a multi-methodological approach, we analyze member comments in Watts Up With That (WUWT), a climate skeptical Facebook group. Quantitative topic modeling revealed that members claim hyperrationality to undermine climate science. Science-based terms were often connected to other topics, such as immigration and LGBTQ+ rights, creating rhetorical constellations that shifted rhetoric from technical spaces into political and ideological ones. These findings have implications for dealing with the challenge of misinformation's circulation on social media.

paragraphs: attributed to science as corrupt or lacking, necessitating a rational resource that is more rational than mainstream scientific argument. We hope that these insights help scholars of communication, rhetoric, the environment, and digital spaces understand more deeply the source of climate skeptics' claims to rationality, in order to respond more effectively when skeptical arguments emerge, both offand online.

Of particular importance to the present study is how the prominence of these reactionary groups and their conservative discourse amplifies the voices and values of primarily white men, who have an outsized impact on policy and media circulation (Kelly, 2020;Zuckerberg, 2018). In the data collection, methods, and analytic frameworks, our study might seem at odds with anti-colonialist, anti-racist projects like those called for by Haas and Frost (2017). But at the risk of centering and elevating these reactionary voices, we argue that this project ultimately contributes to a larger vision of climate justice in that it lays bare the fallacious connection these voices seek to make between reason, objectivity, and climate change denial.

In what follows, we first turn to previous literature that examines the intersection of ideology and climate change as a guiding framework for both the quantitative and rhetorical analyses. We then describe our methodological approaches before presenting our findings. We conclude by reiterating the importance of mixed methods studies, especially for communities involved in public controversy, and exploring implications for the discourse of the WUWT community on digital science and environmental communication.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: Many scholars have explored the connection between ideology and climate denial. For example, Gauchat (2012) used polling data to conclude that both conservative affiliations and religious attendance increased people's skepticism of science as an authority, including over matters of the environment. Hmielowski, Feldman, Myers, Leiserowitz, and Maibach (2014) noted that politically slanted media have an agenda-setting function, whereby "the more Americans use conservative media, the less certain they are that global warming is happening" (p. 878). The change in beliefs is attributed to the lack of trust in climate scientists perpetuated by conservative media (Hmielowski et al., 2014). Similarly, Collomb (2014) noted that the reasons often given to support climate denial are related to conservative and libertarian policies, including an "opposition to regulation" and appeals to the "American way of life" that is largely capitalistic (p. 20).

Themes of conspiracy, anti-elitism, and mistrust of expertise figure prominently in climate denial. Linking ideologies to conspiracy, van der Linden (2015) summarized previous research that conservatives are more likely to hold "conspiratorial views about global warming" (p. 173). For example, Bricker (2013) argued that the Climategate conspiracy was particularly resonant with "conservative ideologies" because it tapped into "existing anti-elitism" held by conservative audiences (pp. 226, 224). The lasting importance of Climategate was also measured by Leiserowitz, Maibach, Roser-Renouf, Smith, and Dawson (2013) who noted that the conspiracy gravely affected "trust in scientists," especially among those who had "a strongly individualistic worldview or political conservative ideology" (p. 818). A rhetorical analysis informed by ideologies attends to "political language" as structuring "public motives" and dictating "belief and behavior" (McGee, 1980, p. 5). As Burke (1966) argued, ideologies make bodies "hop around in certain ways," indicating ideologies' symbolic and material effects (p. 6). This process is iterative, whereby language is evidence of an ideology, and an ideology is reinforced by the use of language. Polarized and harmful rhetoric can therefore have drastic and material consequences. Some might dismiss this polarized rhetoric as having few real consequences, but studies show otherwise. van der Linden (2015) argued that exposure to climate change conspiracies both negatively affected pro-environmental behavior and belief in a scientific consensus about climate change (p. 173). Our analysis continues this important work by exploring how conservative ideologies are deployed as argumentative resources in the discourse of everyday climate deniers. Instead of examining the discourse of established climate change denial groups, we examine the discourse of individuals through the WUWT community. Such an approach can help scholars avoid the rhetorical trap of treating climate deniers as "monolithic and homogenous" (Cagle & Herndl, 2019, p. 4) and instead acknowledge "the multifaceted, complex, and nuanced opposition" still waged against climate science (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019, p. 32). Furthermore, our approach adds to scholarly knowledge on climate change denial rhetoric by examining the role of conservative rhetoric beyond the environment in the performance of a climate denial identity.

sections.header_raw: Methods: distant reading and rhetorical constellations
sections.header_norm: methods distant reading and rhetorical constellations
paragraphs: Because public discourse is increasingly occurring in online spaces, any study of digital media must account for a variety of activities and relationships enabled by the online platform, in addition to the sheer volume of discourse available for analysis. Koteyko (2015) argues that an understanding of audiences' cultural, political, and socioeconomic conditions, along with their ability to use technological resources, "is increasingly necessary for public engagement with science research in our age of digital communication technologies" (p. 184). Koteyko, Jaspal, and Nerlich (2013) and Koteyko (2015) use a method called corpus-assisted discourse analysis, based on Partington (2010), which is characterized by "attention to context -both the situational parameters and the broader sociocultural and political context in which texts making up corpora are embedded" (Koteyko, 2015, p. 188). According to Koteyko (2015), corpus-assisted discourse analysis and webometrics (data from websites regarding use and interactions) allow us to attend to textual levels (including concordances, or measures of word frequency, and collocates, or measures of the frequency at which words occur together), humancomputer interaction, and broad situational and social parameters (p. 188). As Gallagher et al. (2020) note, "distant reading" methods such as the big data audience analysis they describe "can be usefully combined with qualitative methods" (p. 165). In our analysis, we study the two elements of textual levels (concordances and collocates) of WUWT discourse through the textual-analysis program AntConc and the engagement data (likes, reactions, and number of comments) through BirdSong Analytics, and generate topics using the topic modeling program MALLET. Those findings provide a lens through which we view our "constellation approach" to rhetorical analysis.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: While the dataset we study is not as large as those of studies such as Gallagher et al.'s (2020), it is large enough at 17,933 posts and comments that it presents challenges to researchers using methods such as the topic analysis described by Walsh (2013). To engage with our data, we combined a constellation approach with two "distant reading" methods: a list of the most frequently used terms (along with "key words in context") generated by AntConc, and topic modeling using MALLET to identify nine groups of themes. Our use of these distant reading methods is driven by the challenge of making sense of this type of data. The data from Birdsong, a CSV file, offers posts and comments in isolation, each in a separate field, arranged by date of post. While it is possible to follow a conversation in a Facebook page, the presentation of this data makes following an argument more challenging, as the initial post is often a URL with only a few sentences of explanation, if any. The nature of this Facebook group is that the group owner serves an agenda-setting function because group participants do not initiate posts. Thus, while a discussion might stray from an initial post's topic -and there are occasional posters who argue with the initial posts -topics are established by initial posts. Repetition can thus be seen as a measure of engagement. Out of the total of 17,933 items, 4,447 are posts, which are usually links to the WUWT blog, and 13,486 are comments. We did not sort by comment versus post, but both the number and the length of comments can be recognized as measures of engagement with the initial posts. We used AntConc to generate concordances and collocates, or lists of frequently occurring and co-occurring words in the dataset. This analysis counts occurrences throughout the corpus, whether they occur repeatedly in one comment or recur in separate comments throughout the whole.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: The topic modeling program, MALLET, searches for patterns in the use of words across a set of texts (Graham, Weingart, & Milligan, 2012). Topic modeling works on larger units of text, in contrast to collocation, which typically identifies co-occurrences within spans of three to five words (Brookes & McEnery, 2019). Words that co-occur frequently within the same text are "taken by the computer to represent a thematically coherent topic" (Brookes & McEnery, 2019, p. 4). It is then up to the user to assign meanings to those topics. In this case, the unit of analysis for MALLET is the single comment. The MALLET-generated topics are created according to the frequency with which words occur together within the same text, or individual cell (comment). Brookes and McEnery (2019) discuss some of the limitations of topic modeling, including the "naive" model of text (p. 5): the computer assumes the texts are unordered collections of words, and words are stripped from their context. In addition, the researcher sets the number of topics and number of words to search for, which can greatly influence the output. Nevertheless, the process revealed some significant trends that both verified and deepened our rhetorical analysis. The topic modeling approach also addresses the issue of terms occurring frequently in a single post versus across the corpus, a distinction not made by the concordance approach.

It is then up to the user to assign meanings to those topics. In this case, the unit of analysis for MALLET is the single comment. The MALLET-generated topics are created according to the frequency with which words occur together within the same text, or individual cell (comment). Brookes and McEnery (2019) discuss some of the limitations of topic modeling, including the "naive" model of text (p. 5): the computer assumes the texts are unordered collections of words, and words are stripped from their context. In addition, the researcher sets the number of topics and number of words to search for, which can greatly influence the output. Nevertheless, the process revealed some significant trends that both verified and deepened our rhetorical analysis. The topic modeling approach also addresses the issue of terms occurring frequently in a single post versus across the corpus, a distinction not made by the concordance approach.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: We refer to the rhetorical analysis of the WUWT comments and content as a constellation approach because it is based on viewing WUWT's discourse as part of a larger constellation of antienvironmental, skeptical, and conservative discourse present online (refer to Paliewicz & McHendry, 2017). Instead of viewing these argumentative features independently of one another, a constellation approach emphasizes "the recurring patterns of argument interactions" that "heighten [each ideology's] argumentative force" when acting together (Palczewski, 2019, p. 87). In her analysis of arguments against women's suffrage, Palczewski (2019) stated that "recurring arguments are not merely different topoi or lines of argument but, instead, seem to supercharge each other while providing a silhouette of the motive behind the arguments" (p. 87). In other words, argument patterns are not coincidental; they occur because they are linked to underlying ideologies and motivations. Bloomfield (2019a) argued that rhetorical moments are "unique, meaningful, and distinct but also [contribute] to the constellation's overall pattern" that speaks to a larger narrative and ideology (p. 398). Each star in the constellation may itself be a point of argumentation but invoking one of those nodes necessarily calls forth others that are connected to it. Also working with climate change rhetoric, Paliewicz and McHendry (2017) refer to relationships between "discrete and overlapping social forces" in climate change skepticism as "constellations of assemblages," referring to both themes and networked alliances (pp. 5, 3).

In tracing WUWT's rhetorical constellations, both authors examined the data set to tease out the variety of resources that are used to challenge climate science, and how these concepts "intersect" one another and other non-climate topics (Palczewski, 2019, p. 91). The rhetorical analysis was performed by a close reading of the comments on WUWT posts with the most interactions (i.e., likes and comments). In this close reading, both authors tracked on a spreadsheet the presence of topics from the quantitative analyses, in addition to marking expected features such as insults and threats found in previous literature (e.g., Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019). Furthermore, we inductively tracked patterns in how non-climate topics such as politics, economics, national security, social issues, and other ideological markers were deployed within climate skeptical arguments. The tracked themes and patterns were then compared between the authors, and their presence was compared to the findings of the quantitative topic modeling.

By tracking "recurring themes" in both climate arguments and non-climate topics (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019, p. 25), we located identity performances and ideological foundations that constrain WUWT members' ability to adopt or even entertain traditional climate science messages, due to their link to other political issues. The constellation approach, therefore, indicates our attention not only to overt climate topics in the data, such as climate science, but also to seemingly tangential ones, such as gun rights, LGBTQ+ issues, and immigration, to reveal the ideological obstacles to productive climate change communication. When people discuss climate change, it is not only the environment that comes into play; much more is invited to be under discussion, hailed by the various connections between climate and other ideological issues. Taking this constellation of topics into consideration provides insight into the maintenance and proliferation of climate denial online and how it operates to create insular communities resistant to engagement.

Joining the distant-reading methods and the constellation approach to rhetorical analysis is valuable because the circulation of arguments would otherwise be difficult to discern by readers looking at the corpus as a series of spreadsheet cells. In advocating for a mixed-methods qualitative called "crystallization," Ellingson (2009) argues that "strong themes or patterns supported by examples provide a wide angle view of the setting or phenomenon; stories or poems highlight individual experiences, emotions, and expression; critiques shed light on relevant cultural assumptions and constructions; and so on." (p. 8). In our mixed-methods study, our quantitative analysis reveals larger trends, and the qualitative, rhetorical approach makes sense of those elements of "individual experiences, emotions, and expression" -both those captured in the data and our own responses to those strong and sometimes disturbing elements. More specifically, the concordance and topic modeling show "hyperrationality" as highly salient, while the more aggressive assertions of in-group/out-group identity were mostly missed by distant reading but figure prominently in the rhetorical analysis. The integration of findings of the two methodological approaches illustrates the value of integration in mixed-methods research (Tashakkori & Creswell, 2007). This process allows for a much fuller picture of the circulation of polarizing discourses in the WUWT Facebook group.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: We will first discuss findings from the concordance and topic modeling programs, then from the rhetorical analysis. The distant-reading methods offer a high-level view of the dataset, while the rhetorical analysis takes the findings and puts them into a meaningful context through the illustration of ideological constellations. These initial findings show the prominence of science-themed words and reveal the importance of the stance of hyperrationality that is further detailed in the constellation analysis. We use the term hyperrationality to refer to a common stance among these community members that they are more rational than the scientists or experts on global warming, who are framed as irrational by their very commitment to the reality of global warming. The hyperrationality is revealed by findings that show the centrality of science-themed words and topics emerging from the quantitative, computer-generated findings.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: First, the program AntConc was used to generate a concordance sorted by frequency. Once frequently used words were removed (based on a commonly use stoplist, a list of common function words that would otherwise overwhelm the analysis) and terms generated by Facebook metadata were also removed (including months, days of the week, and proper names of members), a list of the top 100 most frequently occurring words was used to generate collocated terms. This analysis was run on the entire set of comments, meaning that the findings do not differentiate between terms occurring repeatedly within one comment or frequently throughout.

Within the ten most frequently occurring words, "climate" and "change" were the top two, along with "warming" and "global." Science-themed words figured prominently, including "science" (#6), "CO2" (#7) and "data" (#9) (Refer to Table 1). Each of the top 20 most frequent words has some relation to scientific evidence or topics (e.g., ice, energy, temperature) except entry 8, "people"; entry 20, "money"; and entry 65, "power." The results suggest the strong emphasis in this Facebook page on appearing, on the surface, to engage with the scientific arguments about climate change, although the collocates, or key words in context, suggest a lack of meaningful engagement with scientific data.

The next step in the AntConc analysis was a list of collocates, or other words that occur primarily in association with the first term. Collocates allow the researcher to select a term and note other terms that occur mostly in close association with the first term. Collocates with the term "data" include terms "untampered/tampered," "selectivity," "interpreted," and "omitted." These modifiers suggest a trend of skepticism toward scientific findings and demonstrate a tendency to subject claims about climate change to interrogation. This association in the dataset seems to confirm Goertzel's (2010) analysis of how conspiracy theories undermine science and demonstrates that what looks like engagement with scientific evidence may likely be a spurious attempt to undermine faith in science. In other words, what may appear to be engagement in scientific debate on the surface is revealed in further analysis to be rejection and modification of scientific information. For example, when collocates of the term "data" are sorted by frequency, the fourth most frequent term (after "temperature," "climate," and "satellite") is "experimental." The "key words in context" list for "data" and "experimental" also suggests a frequent line of argument climate change deniers use: the challenge for scientists to show experimental data over or instead of computer models (Refer to Figure 1: Key Words in Context results for "Data" and "Experimental"). This collocate suggests skepticism toward climate science predictive modeling; our constellation analysis will show how collocations provided a lens we used to organize the constellation approach.

As the list in Table 1 demonstrates, the most frequent terms not associated specifically with science include "people," "money," and "power." The occurrence of these last two terms supports our identification of ways that WUWT members connect environmental topics with ideological arguments. The key words in context for "money" include phrases such as "it's all about the money," and consistent themes of wasting taxpayer money or references to profit by politicians, bureaucrats, and otherwise undefined "special interests" (Cloud, 2020;Tillery, 2018). The term "power" yields more ambiguous results, as it occurs in both the sense of energy power (comments on nuclear power, solar power, and so forth) and the sense of political power, a context-dependent denotation that topic modeling programs cannot account for (Brookes & McEnery, 2019). The combination of "money and power" occurs several times, linking WUWT members' views of climate science as a way for people to achieve money and power. One occurrence of "power" refers to the atmosphere of the UN Climate Change Conference in Paris, which the commenter described as "a nightmarish situation which most people can somehow relate to, although strongly surreal. With an ethereal, 'evil,' omnipotent power floating just beyond the senses." As the topical analysis section describes, "power" in the second sense is poorly defined in these contexts, but WUWT members seem to ascribe power to their political opponents, which would be any scientist, politician, or public figure who discusses anthropogenic global warming as a problem.

The lists of words by frequency and the preliminary collocate data provided a key way to theorize the findings that emerged in the constellation analysis. The constellation analysis had to account for the prominence of seemingly objective words on this list. The data also demonstrates the sheer number of occurrences of science-related words, which also further confirms previous findings that WUWT and similar Facebook groups provide a resource for scientific-seeming arguments to deny global warming (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019). The quantitative tool verifies patterns that emerge in the rhetorical analysis and offers additional insight in terms of the most frequent topics discussed on WUWT. More shocking themes were revealed when both authors coded the data, including aggressive and sometimes violent insults to public figures including Hillary Clinton, Bill Nye, and Michael Mann, or racist and anti-immigrant sentiments associated with climate denial. However, the data overwhelmingly show that most members are posting most frequently about scientific arguments. While it is important to acknowledge and critique the aggressive discourse used in the WUWT community (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019), it is also important to acknowledge that the bulk of WUWT discourse appears, at least on the surface, as topical, rational, and scientific. These findings reveal typical strategies that climate change deniers engage in, as well as the topics that resonate with them; the constellation analysis reveals why these scientific arguments are salient, and how they can evolve into addressing other ideological issues.

The occurrence of these last two terms supports our identification of ways that WUWT members connect environmental topics with ideological arguments. The key words in context for "money" include phrases such as "it's all about the money," and consistent themes of wasting taxpayer money or references to profit by politicians, bureaucrats, and otherwise undefined "special interests" (Cloud, 2020;Tillery, 2018). The term "power" yields more ambiguous results, as it occurs in both the sense of energy power (comments on nuclear power, solar power, and so forth) and the sense of political power, a context-dependent denotation that topic modeling programs cannot account for (Brookes & McEnery, 2019). The combination of "money and power" occurs several times, linking WUWT members' views of climate science as a way for people to achieve money and power. One occurrence of "power" refers to the atmosphere of the UN Climate Change Conference in Paris, which the commenter described as "a nightmarish situation which most people can somehow relate to, although strongly surreal. With an ethereal, 'evil,' omnipotent power floating just beyond the senses." As the topical analysis section describes, "power" in the second sense is poorly defined in these contexts, but WUWT members seem to ascribe power to their political opponents, which would be any scientist, politician, or public figure who discusses anthropogenic global warming as a problem.

The lists of words by frequency and the preliminary collocate data provided a key way to theorize the findings that emerged in the constellation analysis. The constellation analysis had to account for the prominence of seemingly objective words on this list. The data also demonstrates the sheer number of occurrences of science-related words, which also further confirms previous findings that WUWT and similar Facebook groups provide a resource for scientific-seeming arguments to deny global warming (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019). The quantitative tool verifies patterns that emerge in the rhetorical analysis and offers additional insight in terms of the most frequent topics discussed on WUWT. More shocking themes were revealed when both authors coded the data, including aggressive and sometimes violent insults to public figures including Hillary Clinton, Bill Nye, and Michael Mann, or racist and anti-immigrant sentiments associated with climate denial. However, the data overwhelmingly show that most members are posting most frequently about scientific arguments. While it is important to acknowledge and critique the aggressive discourse used in the WUWT community (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019), it is also important to acknowledge that the bulk of WUWT discourse appears, at least on the surface, as topical, rational, and scientific. These findings reveal typical strategies that climate change deniers engage in, as well as the topics that resonate with them; the constellation analysis reveals why these scientific arguments are salient, and how they can evolve into addressing other ideological issues.
sections.preview_head: First, the program AntConc was used to generate a concordance sorted by frequency. Once frequently used words were removed (based on a commonly use stoplist, a list of common function words that would otherwise overwhelm the analysis) and terms generated by Facebook metadata were also removed (including months, days of the week, and proper names of members), a list of the top 100 most frequently occurring words was used to generate collocated terms. This analysis was run on the entire set of comments, meaning that the findings do not differentiate between terms occurring repeatedly within one comment or frequently throughout.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: To supplement the concordance and collocation data, we also ran a topic-modeling program on this data set, using the single comment as our unit of analysis. In this study, parameters were set at 20 words and 10 topics, given that we were working with a relatively small dataset in comparison to analyses like those done by Gallagher et al. (2020) and Brookes and McEnery (2019). The process of running the topic modeling is iterative, as we added new stopwords to our list, including numbers, site names like "youtube" that occur within links and metadata, and first names. After running MALLET with 10 and 20 topics, we generated a list of nine-word sets (Supplementary File 1) that mapped onto meaningful topics (see Table 2).

One key finding mirrors the finding from the AntConc analysis that most of the posts do focus on specific scientific topics, although it is not possible to say anything about the quality of that engagement. 1 For example, topics 1, 2, and 3 are focused on disputes about global temperatures, whether the Earth is warming or not, or whether any warming is a function of natural processes, whether Earth-based or based on solar cycles, as revealed by the comments sorted into those topics. Other topics, including topic 5 and 9, claim climate scientists cherry-pick data, or are hopelessly enmeshed in politicians like Al Gore and organizations like the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change (IPCC), both of which are intensely distrusted by this community. The prominence of this set of terms in the topics appears neutral on its face, but linking the terms in topic 9 with the IPCC and Al Gore confirm Tillery's (2018) identification of common topoi about environmental scientists being biased. Neutral-seeming terms like "proof" or "evidence" occur mainly in these contexts: "evidence" shows up in comments such as "My professors [of geology] were highly skeptical of these claims [of human-caused global warming] and believed there was not solid empirical evidence." The focus on politics and policy is also evident, as topic 7 reveals, which also demonstrates that "Trump" and "Obama" regularly co-occur in this theme. The concordance, collocates, and topic modeling helped to set the context for our constellation analysis. The prominence of topic 2, the co-occurrence of words including CO2, earth, solar, heat, ice, sea, years, weather, and study, offered further evidence that the WUWT members engage with scientific-seeming content quite actively. The prominence of these terms and topics in the dataset helped frame the analysis, as it clearly reveals the importance of the hyperrationality stance to this community. Topic 5 reveals that the unnamed individual, who consistently writes posts in the group arguing that the content posted by WUWT is either scientifically inaccurate or a misrepresentation of the science, prompts many responses accusing the user of being irrational, unscientific, and alarmist.

Topics 4, 5, 6, and 8 illustrate the theme of ideological arguments. One finding the topic analysis reveals is the salience of a particular humanist-seeming argument: that any measures to mitigate climate change are harmful to poor countries and poor people in more wealthy countries. This argument allows for an appearance of empathy and compassion, notably lacking in much of the other discourse on this page. More common is the set of arguments suggested by 4, 7, and 8, circulating around specific political figures or institutions that are accused by climate-science deniers of being hopelessly politicized. This analysis reveals the challenges of confronting misinformation on a platform like Facebook: a large part of the engagement on these pages comes from arguments that conflate science and politics and may seem on their surface to be genuine engagement with climate science.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: The themes uncovered in the rhetorical analysis of WUWT's Facebook page point to an overarching conservative ideology that doubles down on perceived standards of scientific inquiry and links climate change activism to other political issues. Claims to hyperrationality found in the quantitative modeling were also borne out in the rhetorical analysis. In addition to analyzing how rationality is deployed by undermining the perceived rationality of climate scientists, environmentalists, and climate advocates, we also examine how rationality is inextricably linked to WUWT's political ideology, which, in turn, leads to the integration of other political and social issues into environmental arguments. While we separate claims to hyperrationality and ideologies here to organize the analysis, these two themes work together to support a meta-narrative that codes scientific and pro-environmental arguments as liberal polemic.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: Emerging prominently in the data were appeals to WUWT members and other skeptics as more rational (and scientific) than scientists. We call this rhetorical strategy the establishment of hyperrationality, which we previously defined as the creation of an alternative standard of rationality that supersedes scientific rationality. Goertzel (2010) explored a similar idea in an examination of how conspiracy theories undermine all aspects of science by "demand[ing] immediate, comprehensive and definitive answers to all questions" (p. 493). Without those answers, science can be framed as dismissing its commitment to rationality and thereby as an irrational decision-making resource. Questioning the rationality of climate scientists is an established strategy of climate deniers (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019;Ceccarelli, 2011;Knight & Greenberg, 2011), especially when skeptics make use of outdated Baconian definitions of science (Taylor, 1992).

In proposing that climate skeptics are more rational than scientists, WUWT members directly commented on what they viewed as rational behaviors. One commenter characterized "true skeptics" as "critical thinkers" who could not be duped by climate scientists and would not "submi[t] to their delusions." Another WUWT member embraced the label of "Science doubting." The commenter wrote that doubt "is essential within true science. It can be taken as a compliment." This commenter appealed to scientific standards of skepticism and questioning as identity labels that heightened the commenter's own scientific credibility.

Climate skeptics refuted climate change alarmism and appealed to their own rationality by providing their own evidence. An oft-repeated claim was that carbon dioxide benefits plants, a common climate skeptic argument that McCright and Dunlap (2003) call, "the social construction of non-problematicity" (p. 351). The WUWT moderator posted, "New research from a Florida State University scientist has revealed a surprising relationship between surging atmospheric carbon dioxide and flower blooms in a remote tropical forest." This argument also appeared in comments, such as one WUWT member arguing, "CO2 is not a green house gas and is not a pollutant. It is the basic building block of life for all living things on Earth." Similar to previous findings about the WUWT community (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019), these arguments were often missing context, such as the upper limit on when too much carbon dioxide becomes harmful and other factors that contribute to plant growth that will be adversely affected by increased carbon dioxide (Hay-Chapman, 2016). Thus, these arguments appear to be rational and to make use of scientific vocabulary, but they are typically assertions that lack full reasoning and evidence.

For example, one commenter focused on the benefits of warming on grapes, arguing that "Past warmings have been great for Grapes. Roman Warming saw grape growing far north of England, maybe Greenland will be the Napa of the future." Not only does this commenter not account for what the rest of the world might look like if Greenland becomes hot and dry enough to grow grapes, but the commenter also seems to elevate the importance of grape production over the immense damage to other plant and animal life that would occur with so much warming (Hay-Chapman, 2016). Using both an offensive portmanteau (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019) and an appeal to non-problematicity (McCright & Dunlap, 2003), one commenter wrote, "Do LIBTARDS HAVE ANY IDEA THAT CO2 BENEFITS PLANTS ???" (Emphasis in original). This commenter frames this knowledge as obvious and those who deny it or claim that carbon dioxide is harmful as irrational. Therefore, believers in climate change, particularly liberals, are irrational. Connecting climate change to politics was also revealed in MALLET topic 7, but this method did not capture the ad hominem and insults that accompanied them.

For climate skeptics, the truth about climate change is obvious, meaning that anyone who contradicts it is clearly motivated by malicious intentions or illogic. One commenter called climate advocates "insane." Another emphasized emotions as counter to rationality: "people seem to get weirdly emotional about climate science and lose the capacity for critical thinking and rational debate." These assertions echo the common refrain that environmentalists are "hysterical" (Killingsworth & Palmer, 1995, p. 1). This hysteria was also characterized as a cult-like devotion as demonstrated by these comments:
Finally we're making a dent in this climate catastrophe religious fanaticism. All of the data is against them and they still cling to their religion.
Blending science and religion failed in the Middle Ages. You couldn't contradict 'science' back then either.
[Climate scientists] have to pay proper obeisance to the AGW religion.

Comparing climate change science to faith undermined the perceived authority of scientists and the evidence supporting scientists' beliefs. Conflating climate science and religion works to "reduce science to faith rather than fact" and "deconstructs the authority of scientists, their scholarly rigor, and their observational and logical character" (Bloomfield & Lake, 2015, pp. 386, 387). In addition to bolstering commenters' own rationality, strategies such as these undermined the rationality of climate scientists and climate advocates.

As well as characterizing scientists as unscientific because of ignorance or hysteria, WUWT members also discounted the credibility of climate scientists by ascribing ulterior motives to their irrationality (Cloud, 2020). The theme of "follow the money" (Tillery, 2018) appeared frequently in the WUWT comments, where climate scientists are positioned as colluding with governments and agencies to control people's finances. One commenter surmised that climate scientists scramble to make themselves appear credible because if society no longer trusts climate scientists, "Governments then lose their excuse to impose green taxes." Former Vice President Al Gore was a frequent target of monetary conspiracy accusations. One commenter wrote, Al Gore "has become a bilionare [sic] off his gullible minions. It's all about the money." Using another portmanteau (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019), one commenter argued, "Gorebal Warming had always been about using the fear of the boogeyman to seize money from the producers to give to the parasites with the limited amount of effort." The combining of his last name and "global" emphasizes the influence and control that this commenter ascribes to Gore's environmental activism. One commenter summarized the group's skepticism of Gore thusly, Follow the money. Nothing changes. Gore has become a billionaire using climate as his very own money printing machine at the expense of every hard working family. I don't believe he really believes the nonsense he's spewing but it makes him rich and that's his real agenda! One WUWT member targeted the government as the source of financial conspiracy, noting, Here's a tip for you. When studying so called scientific data, search a little deeper to find out who paid for the research. If the study was funded by government mark it with a G. If it's privatly [sic] funded mark it with a P. Government funding has an agenda. Always follow the money.

While scholars are often skeptical of privately funded research as potentially biased based on the interest of the funder, this skeptic flips that bias by arguing that only privately funded research is free from the bias of institutions that have been invaded by environmentalists. These comments are examples of money and power, which emerged in MALLET topic 6. The deployment of these arguments calls into question the motivations and perceived biases of climate science. Ceccarelli (2011) argued that climate skeptics turn the values of scientific inquiry, namely "openmindedness, freedom of inquiry, and fairness" (p. 198), against climate science by claiming that skeptics have been shut out of conversations. One commenter argued that it fits the agenda of climate scientists to repress discussion because, "If debate is allowed, why people might follow the scientific facts, rather than the political agenda." One WUWT member critiqued what they saw as apparent hypocrisy in the behaviors of climate change advocates:

People like these are tolerant and open minded and promote diversity and seek to be inclusive till you ask the first question then you wind up in a dungeon someplace with chain-link fences and razor wire and there are spotlights and men with guns. In the end there are showers and crematoriums. Every time.

Connecting the promotion of environmental policies to a slippery slope that ends in the Holocaust, this commenter proposes that environmentalists can be violent, controlling, and motivated by malicious intentions. Furthermore, climate skeptics are painted as victims of an oppressive and authoritarian system.

Additionally, WUWT members highlighted the increased need for their presence and activism against the powerful forces of environmentalism that they saw seeping into politics. Overall, however, they portrayed optimism that their version of rationality would win out. Because climate skeptics believe that they have the truth of the matter, they believe that their beliefs will eventually overthrow mainstream climate science and become accepted as the norm (Bloomfield, 2019b;Ceccarelli, 2011). One commenter wrote, "It's apparent now [that] the consensus is turning toward rational science rather than politically motivated fear." Another echoed this idea noting, "It's almost as though rational inquiry is starting to have its effect on political dogma. Yay!" In bifurcating rationalism from politics and associating climate skepticism with rationalism and politics with environmentalism, WUWT members drew climate skepticism closer to "real" science and relegated environmentalism to irrational politics. This connection is taken up further in the next section that highlights the conservative ideology that underlies climate skepticism and works against the perceived liberal environmental agenda.

As well as characterizing scientists as unscientific because of ignorance or hysteria, WUWT members also discounted the credibility of climate scientists by ascribing ulterior motives to their irrationality (Cloud, 2020). The theme of "follow the money" (Tillery, 2018) appeared frequently in the WUWT comments, where climate scientists are positioned as colluding with governments and agencies to control people's finances. One commenter surmised that climate scientists scramble to make themselves appear credible because if society no longer trusts climate scientists, "Governments then lose their excuse to impose green taxes." Former Vice President Al Gore was a frequent target of monetary conspiracy accusations. One commenter wrote, Al Gore "has become a bilionare [sic] off his gullible minions. It's all about the money." Using another portmanteau (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019), one commenter argued, "Gorebal Warming had always been about using the fear of the boogeyman to seize money from the producers to give to the parasites with the limited amount of effort." The combining of his last name and "global" emphasizes the influence and control that this commenter ascribes to Gore's environmental activism. One commenter summarized the group's skepticism of Gore thusly, Follow the money. Nothing changes. Gore has become a billionaire using climate as his very own money printing machine at the expense of every hard working family. I don't believe he really believes the nonsense he's spewing but it makes him rich and that's his real agenda! If it's privatly [sic] funded mark it with a P. Government funding has an agenda. Always follow the money.

Ceccarelli (2011) argued that climate skeptics turn the values of scientific inquiry, namely "openmindedness, freedom of inquiry, and fairness" (p. 198), against climate science by claiming that skeptics have been shut out of conversations. One commenter argued that it fits the agenda of climate scientists to repress discussion because, "If debate is allowed, why people might follow the scientific facts, rather than the political agenda." One WUWT member critiqued what they saw as apparent hypocrisy in the behaviors of climate change advocates:

In the end there are showers and crematoriums. Every time.

Connecting the promotion of environmental policies to a slippery slope that ends in the Holocaust, this commenter proposes that environmentalists can be violent, controlling, and motivated by malicious intentions. Furthermore, climate skeptics are painted as victims of an oppressive and authoritarian system.

Additionally, WUWT members highlighted the increased need for their presence and activism against the powerful forces of environmentalism that they saw seeping into politics. Overall, however, they portrayed optimism that their version of rationality would win out. Because climate skeptics believe that they have the truth of the matter, they believe that their beliefs will eventually overthrow mainstream climate science and become accepted as the norm (Bloomfield, 2019b;Ceccarelli, 2011). One commenter wrote, "It's apparent now [that] the consensus is turning toward rational science rather than politically motivated fear." Another echoed this idea noting, "It's almost as though rational inquiry is starting to have its effect on political dogma. Yay!" In bifurcating rationalism from politics and associating climate skepticism with rationalism and politics with environmentalism, WUWT members drew climate skepticism closer to "real" science and relegated environmentalism to irrational politics. This connection is taken up further in the next section that highlights the conservative ideology that underlies climate skepticism and works against the perceived liberal environmental agenda.

Emerging prominently in the data were appeals to WUWT members and other skeptics as more rational (and scientific) than scientists. We call this rhetorical strategy the establishment of hyperrationality, which we previously defined as the creation of an alternative standard of rationality that supersedes scientific rationality. Goertzel (2010) explored a similar idea in an examination of how conspiracy theories undermine all aspects of science by "demand[ing] immediate, comprehensive and definitive answers to all questions" (p. 493). Without those answers, science can be framed as dismissing its commitment to rationality and thereby as an irrational decision-making resource. Questioning the rationality of climate scientists is an established strategy of climate deniers (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019;Ceccarelli, 2011;Knight & Greenberg, 2011), especially when skeptics make use of outdated Baconian definitions of science (Taylor, 1992).

In proposing that climate skeptics are more rational than scientists, WUWT members directly commented on what they viewed as rational behaviors. One commenter characterized "true skeptics" as "critical thinkers" who could not be duped by climate scientists and would not "submi[t] to their delusions." Another WUWT member embraced the label of "Science doubting." The commenter wrote that doubt "is essential within true science. It can be taken as a compliment." This commenter appealed to scientific standards of skepticism and questioning as identity labels that heightened the commenter's own scientific credibility.

Climate skeptics refuted climate change alarmism and appealed to their own rationality by providing their own evidence. An oft-repeated claim was that carbon dioxide benefits plants, a common climate skeptic argument that McCright and Dunlap (2003) call, "the social construction of non-problematicity" (p. 351). The WUWT moderator posted, "New research from a Florida State University scientist has revealed a surprising relationship between surging atmospheric carbon dioxide and flower blooms in a remote tropical forest." sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
paragraphs: Claims to hyperrationality are bolstered by connecting the topic of climate change to a variety of environmental, social, and political topics. For WUWT members, a conservative ideology serves as the logic for determining what is rational and what is not across a variety of issues. While the quantitative data show that WUWT members tend to discuss the scientific merits of climate change, it was not uncommon in the rhetorical analysis to recognize conspiracy ideations that linked climate change to other issues. These discursive fragments are instances of the underlying ideology, which guides beliefs and behaviors on climate change. WUWT members ascribe to the belief that the climate conversation is rife with ulterior motives and conspiracies, so they reason that there are other areas in addition to the environment in which this irrationality proliferates. Furthermore, the political language that bolsters denial of climate science proliferates into these other connected topics as collective evidence of irrationality, conspiracy, and liberal agendas.

For climate skeptics, bringing up other topics amplified the dangers of environmentalism as threatening other ways of life. While they do not consider their own loyalty to conservative politics to be baggage, WUWT members link climate science and advocacy for the environment to other anti-conservative policies as further reason to oppose them. In other words, climate skeptics do not recognize the controversy over climate change as a one-issue discussion. Instead, advocacy around climate change may appear to threaten "sustained economic growth, the free market, nationality sovereignty, and the continued abolition of government regulations -key goals promoted by the conservative movement" (McCright & Dunlap, 2003, p. 353). It makes sense, therefore, that in challenging what they perceive to be a politicized issue, other political concerns seem relevant to include as part of the conversation. Bringing in these other issues may also help to build the community's sense of identity in locating multiple points of commonality and agreement beyond their shared skepticism of climate change. The quantity of links, however tenuous, increases the argument's strength and the perception of the skeptical position's clarity and self-consistency. The invocation of political language works in tandem with appeals to hyperrationality to form a rhetorical constellation that shifts the conversation to topics where there is more legitimate discussion and debate than climate change.

To demonstrate the constellation in action, we focus on the commenters' expressions of the connections they drew between liberal policies and climate science. For example, WUWT members linked climate science conclusions to the left's support of (undocumented) immigration, LGBTQ+ rights, intersectionality, and gun control. This constellation positions climate change as just one policy in the "the empty fantasies of confused leftists" (Killingsworth & Palmer, 1995, p. 8) that should be attacked as a coherent whole. Watts, the WUWT moderator, posted an article about people tweeting #Irma who linked Hurricane Irma to climate change. Watts described the tweets as "hatred on display," noting, "I blame it on Trump Derangement Syndrome, oh wait, some were deranged before he got elected." Watts directly links climate change reporting to liberal ideologies, and more specifically, refers to Trump Derangement Syndrome, which describes a particular brand of hysteria caused by Trump that conservatives ascribe to liberals. Echoing Trump's claim during the final 2016 Presidential Debate that Hillary Clinton is a "nasty woman," one WUWT commenter described Canadian Green Party leader Elizabeth May thusly: "This woman is quite the nasty . . . Horrible woman." Although Obama and Clinton were no longer in prominent positions of political power at the time comments were posted, WUWT members were still concerned that Obama and Clinton were seeking to "rule the world." One commenter wrote that "Hillary is a traitor" and attributed full responsibility for the September 2012 attack on a U.S. diplomatic post in Benghazi and "lost billions from the State Dept" to Clinton. Notably, these examples express latent sexism in the group that circulates unchecked, by portraying women as uniquely untrustworthy. Liberal political leaders were thus recognized as part and parcel of the larger liberal, environmental agenda, which included, as a WUWT member noted, "Hollywood celebrity scientists," as evidence of the pervasive connection between liberalism and climate science. While the topical analysis showed frequent mention of male politicians such as Trump and Obama, the rhetorical analysis also shows pointed critiques, while infrequent, of women involved in climate advocacy.

WUWT members largely shared skepticism of government intervention and regulation, except when it came to border control. Commenters expressed worries that conservative values, such as neoliberalism and national autonomy, were at stake in climate deliberation. For example, one commenter wrote that climate advocacy will lead to "socialist views [of] using the military to educate and restrain the undesirables" who do not submit to government control. Control was also mentioned by another commenter who wrote, "The only people who view fossil fuels as a dead end are people who dream of controlling the energy, food, healthcare, housing, location, free speech, life span we could go on, of other people." Commenters connected issues of control to government overreach and the perceived liberal agenda to have the government run all aspects of people's lives.

In the growing constellation of climate change, politicians, celebrities, sexism, and government overreach, commenters also brought up social issues. One commenter proposed that advocates of climate change policies and LGBTQ+ rights share the ulterior motive of promoting population control: "The control freaks are constantly at it. Maybe we should all become homosexual-that would really reduce the birthrate." Other commenters echoed a disdain for immigrant populations and their belief that these policies were motivated by power grabs: Does [anyone] think migrants are going to give a crap about the environment? Judging by the trash they leave all over the border when they illegally cross, the answer is a resounding no. If progressives truly cared about the environment, they'd stop immigration tomorrow. But they want an electoral super-majority that can only come by displacing native born Americans.

In expressing this racist argument, the commenter linked environmentalism to political control through vote manipulation and implied that environmentalists are hypocritical in their dual support of environmentalism and immigration. While WUWT members viewed their own positions as coherent, environmental arguments were portrayed as contradictory and misguided.

Although rare, other racist themes of purity and protecting Western/white culture were also present, calling into question any consideration of these groups as rational. One commenter proposed that the reason environmentalists support population control is to increase immigration: "when low birthrates happen, we need to import masses of immigrants. It's not about the climate, but about destroying Western civilisation. [sic] They're just using climate to try and enact the change when other methods failed." For this commenter, climate change advocacy is not solely nor even primarily about the environment, but about the destruction of Western values, which the commenter perceives as at risk with increased immigration. On an article about a California bill that would allow limiting the publication of climate skeptical beliefs, one commenter wrote, "Up next, a bill banning Republicans. Then whites." This WUWT member's slippery slope fallacy includes political and racial persecution against climate skeptics, implying both that climate skeptics are largely white and that whiteness itself is currently under attack. Another commenter echoed the persecution of climate skeptics in noting, "If you replaced 'Climate Change Denier' with 'Jew,' 'Christian,' 'Muslim,' 'Gay' or the 'N' word it would be no worse than this." The WUWT group creates a sense of identity and community by positioning itself as under attack from an environmental threat. This framework of war simultaneously justifies the group's presence and legitimizes their own aggressive and threatening responses (Bloomfield, 2019b).

This type of discourse might not be the most frequently posted in the WUWT community, but its presence, in any quantity, poses ideological obstacles to climate science and links climate skepticism to other harmful ideologies such as sexism and racism. Taken together, hyperrationality arguments and ideological arguments form a compelling constellation that motivates members of the WUWT community to maintain their identity and reject mainstream scientific conclusions about the severity and urgency of climate change. Furthermore, linking environmentalism to other issues creates an expansive constellation that covers a variety of political and social topics. In this way, the WUWT Facebook community, and perhaps other similar groups online, are not merely climate denial groups, but ideologically driven political actors that have mobilized against what they perceive to be multiple threats to their identity and worldview, while on the surface appearing to focus on climate change.

Appealing to hyperrationality and questioning the rationality of climate scientists and advocates lent legitimacy to WUWT member opinions on all issues up for debate. Spanning the environment, immigration, LGBTQ+ issues, taxation, and politics, the rhetoric of WUWT members launches a powerful attack on any topic that they perceive as challenging their conservative values. The climate skeptical constellation deployed by WUWT draws its power simultaneously from being a unified collection and from individual issues, each deserving its own attention. To push the constellation metaphor even further, one might say that the environment is the Polaris or North Star that shines the brightest but is also only part of the Little Dipper constellation. To address one star in the constellation is to invoke the entire assemblage of arguments, albeit in varying degrees and frequencies, that constitutes much of the climate skeptical community's rhetorical force.

While they do not consider their own loyalty to conservative politics to be baggage, WUWT members link climate science and advocacy for the environment to other anti-conservative policies as further reason to oppose them. Instead, advocacy around climate change may appear to threaten "sustained economic growth, the free market, nationality sovereignty, and the continued abolition of government regulations -key goals promoted by the conservative movement" (McCright & Dunlap, 2003, p. 353). It makes sense, therefore, that in challenging what they perceive to be a politicized issue, other political concerns seem relevant to include as part of the conversation. Bringing in these other issues may also help to build the community's sense of identity in locating multiple points of commonality and agreement beyond their shared skepticism of climate change. The quantity of links, however tenuous, increases the argument's strength and the perception of the skeptical position's clarity and self-consistency. The invocation of political language works in tandem with appeals to hyperrationality to form a rhetorical constellation that shifts the conversation to topics where there is more legitimate discussion and debate than climate change.

To demonstrate the constellation in action, we focus on the commenters' expressions of the connections they drew between liberal policies and climate science. For example, WUWT members linked climate science conclusions to the left's support of (undocumented) immigration, LGBTQ+ rights, intersectionality, and gun control. This constellation positions climate change as just one policy in the "the empty fantasies of confused leftists" (Killingsworth & Palmer, 1995, p. 8) that should be attacked as a coherent whole. Watts, the WUWT moderator, posted an article about people tweeting #Irma who linked Hurricane Irma to climate change. Watts described the tweets as "hatred on display," noting, "I blame it on Trump Derangement Syndrome, oh wait, some were deranged before he got elected." Watts directly links climate change reporting to liberal ideologies, and more specifically, refers to Trump Derangement Syndrome, which describes a particular brand of hysteria caused by Trump that conservatives ascribe to liberals. Echoing Trump's claim during the final 2016 Presidential Debate that Hillary Clinton is a "nasty woman," one WUWT commenter described Canadian Green Party leader Elizabeth May thusly: "This woman is quite the nasty . . . Horrible woman." Although Obama and Clinton were no longer in prominent positions of political power at the time comments were posted, WUWT members were still concerned that Obama and Clinton were seeking to "rule the world." One commenter wrote that "Hillary is a traitor" and attributed full responsibility for the September 2012 attack on a U.S. diplomatic post in Benghazi and "lost billions from the State Dept" to Clinton. Notably, these examples express latent sexism in the group that circulates unchecked, by portraying women as uniquely untrustworthy. Liberal political leaders were thus recognized as part and parcel of the larger liberal, environmental agenda, which included, as a WUWT member noted, "Hollywood celebrity scientists," as evidence of the pervasive connection between liberalism and climate science. While the topical analysis showed frequent mention of male politicians such as Trump and Obama, the rhetorical analysis also shows pointed critiques, while infrequent, of women involved in climate advocacy.

WUWT members largely shared skepticism of government intervention and regulation, except when it came to border control. Commenters expressed worries that conservative values, such as neoliberalism and national autonomy, were at stake in climate deliberation. For example, one commenter wrote that climate advocacy will lead to "socialist views [of] using the military to educate and restrain the undesirables" who do not submit to government control. Control was also mentioned by another commenter who wrote, "The only people who view fossil fuels as a dead end are people who dream of controlling the energy, food, healthcare, housing, location, free speech, life span we could go on, of other people." Commenters connected issues of control to government overreach and the perceived liberal agenda to have the government run all aspects of people's lives.

In the growing constellation of climate change, politicians, celebrities, sexism, and government overreach, commenters also brought up social issues. One commenter proposed that advocates of climate change policies and LGBTQ+ rights share the ulterior motive of promoting population control: "The control freaks are constantly at it. Maybe we should all become homosexual-that would really reduce the birthrate." Other commenters echoed a disdain for immigrant populations and their belief that these policies were motivated by power grabs: Does [anyone] think migrants are going to give a crap about the environment? Judging by the trash they leave all over the border when they illegally cross, the answer is a resounding no. If progressives truly cared about the environment, they'd stop immigration tomorrow. But they want an electoral super-majority that can only come by displacing native born Americans.

In expressing this racist argument, the commenter linked environmentalism to political control through vote manipulation and implied that environmentalists are hypocritical in their dual support of environmentalism and immigration. While WUWT members viewed their own positions as coherent, environmental arguments were portrayed as contradictory and misguided.

Although rare, other racist themes of purity and protecting Western/white culture were also present, calling into question any consideration of these groups as rational. One commenter proposed that the reason environmentalists support population control is to increase immigration: "when low birthrates happen, we need to import masses of immigrants. It's not about the climate, but about destroying Western civilisation. [sic] They're just using climate to try and enact the change when other methods failed." For this commenter, climate change advocacy is not solely nor even primarily about the environment, but about the destruction of Western values, which the commenter perceives as at risk with increased immigration. On an article about a California bill that would allow limiting the publication of climate skeptical beliefs, one commenter wrote, "Up next, a bill banning Republicans. Then whites." This WUWT member's slippery slope fallacy includes political and racial persecution against climate skeptics, implying both that climate skeptics are largely white and that whiteness itself is currently under attack. Another commenter echoed the persecution of climate skeptics in noting, "If you replaced 'Climate Change Denier' with 'Jew,' 'Christian,' 'Muslim,' 'Gay' or the 'N' word it would be no worse than this." The WUWT group creates a sense of identity and community by positioning itself as under attack from an environmental threat. This framework of war simultaneously justifies the group's presence and legitimizes their own aggressive and threatening responses (Bloomfield, 2019b).

This type of discourse might not be the most frequently posted in the WUWT community, but its presence, in any quantity, poses ideological obstacles to climate science and links climate skepticism to other harmful ideologies such as sexism and racism. Taken together, hyperrationality arguments and ideological arguments form a compelling constellation that motivates members of the WUWT community to maintain their identity and reject mainstream scientific conclusions about the severity and urgency of climate change. Furthermore, linking environmentalism to other issues creates an expansive constellation that covers a variety of political and social topics. In this way, the WUWT Facebook community, and perhaps other similar groups online, are not merely climate denial groups, but ideologically driven political actors that have mobilized against what they perceive to be multiple threats to their identity and worldview, while on the surface appearing to focus on climate change.

Appealing to hyperrationality and questioning the rationality of climate scientists and advocates lent legitimacy to WUWT member opinions on all issues up for debate. Spanning the environment, immigration, LGBTQ+ issues, taxation, and politics, the rhetoric of WUWT members launches a powerful attack on any topic that they perceive as challenging their conservative values. The climate skeptical constellation deployed by WUWT draws its power simultaneously from being a unified collection and from individual issues, each deserving its own attention. To push the constellation metaphor even further, one might say that the environment is the Polaris or North Star that shines the brightest but is also only part of the Little Dipper constellation. To address one star in the constellation is to invoke the entire assemblage of arguments, albeit in varying degrees and frequencies, that constitutes much of the climate skeptical community's rhetorical force.
sections.preview_head: Claims to hyperrationality are bolstered by connecting the topic of climate change to a variety of environmental, social, and political topics. For WUWT members, a conservative ideology serves as the logic for determining what is rational and what is not across a variety of issues. While the quantitative data show that WUWT members tend to discuss the scientific merits of climate change, it was not uncommon in the rhetorical analysis to recognize conspiracy ideations that linked climate change to other issues.

sections.paper_id: Tillery and Bloomfield - 2022 - Hyperrationality and Rhetorical Constellations in Digital Climate Change Denial A Multi-Methodologi
sections.header_raw: Implications for digital climate discourse research
sections.header_norm: implications for digital climate discourse research
paragraphs: This study has deployed multiple methods to study the wicked problem of climate change and how climate skeptics leverage digital spaces to doubt climate science (refer to Cagle & Tillery, 2015). By combining quantitative and rhetorical methods, this study reveals both the surface-level prominence of seemingly rational, science-oriented themes in WUWT's posts and the deeper, interconnected layers of politics that drive the community and its claims to rationality. To avoid the potential emphases and corresponding deflections based on one's methodological choices, we urge more TPC scholars, especially those studying scientific topics such as climate change, to employ multiple methodologies in their research. Our methodological choices are even more pertinent as the field centers interdisciplinarity, which necessitates diverse perspectives and approaches.

There are many findings in this study about the state of science and environmental communication, especially online, that should prompt concern. As our analysis demonstrates, appeals to hyperrationality help to create a self-closing loop by arming members with technical-sounding arguments that can be recirculated in other spoken and written contexts. These rhetorical practices also function to reaffirm group membership, particularly in this overwhelmingly male group (about 87.6% identify as male, as reported by BirdSong Analytics) where rationality is assumed to be masculine and hysterical emotionalism is assumed to characterize femininized liberals and irrational scientists (Killingsworth & Palmer, 1995). "Reason" functions as a style, an inventional resource when paired with technical terms, and an argumentative stance.

These findings should not overshadow or ignore, however, evidence of how other digital technical communities are leveraging the affordances of digital spaces for productive deliberation (Cagle & Herndl, 2019), "deep communal identification" (Holladay, 2017, p. 12), and sharing scientific information (Cagle & Tillery, 2017). Furthermore, our research is not intended to over-emphasize skeptical attitudes toward climate change; the most recent Yale Program on Climate Change survey (Leiserowitz et al., 2019) found that 6 in 10 Americans agree with the scientific consensus that climate change is real and anthropogenic. Rather, this work illuminates how, similar to climate change itself, climate skeptics are multifaceted, complex, and interconnected in ways we are still exploring and analyzing.

In this piece, we focus on the circulation of misinformation and recurrent lines of argument in social media as both its own problem and as a symptom of a larger pattern of discourse where online platforms including Reddit (Cagle & Herndl, 2015), Twitter (Bowdon, 2014), and Facebook (Bloomfield & Tillery, 2019) not only provide spaces for conversation but also shape the discourses and views of participants in ways that can be problematic and detrimental to deliberative ends. By learning more about how these communities spread misinformation and what information is being spread, TPC scholars can support future research into refutation and advise effective strategies for environmental communicators, especially in digital spaces.
